title: The Charter Was the Strongest Instrument on Paper. First It Had to Prove It Applied at All.
setting:
Slavno is a river town in the north of the Republic of Valmiria, a Central-European state that joined the European Union in the 2004 enlargement. On a quiet street called Kestenova, behind a chain-link fence, stands a disused textile warehouse the town's Tatar Muslim community bought three years ago and hoped to turn into a mosque. The Valmirian Tatars are not new arrivals: their ancestors were settled here as border cavalry some six hundred years ago, and they have prayed, married, and buried their dead in this valley for longer than most of the surrounding villages have existed. They are a distinct people as much as a distinct faith, with their own surnames, their own dialect, and about eleven thousand souls in a country of four million. Emine Sabitova, forty-four, a hospital midwife, chairs the Slavno Muslim Foundation. For nine years the congregation of roughly three hundred has prayed in the low basement of a former trade-union hall, ducking the pipes. The warehouse was paid for the slow way, and the conversion permit had cleared two of its three municipal hurdles when the national mood turned.

Riding a long anti-immigration wave, the National Line bloc in the Valmirian National Assembly introduced the Act on the Suspension of the Establishment of New Places of Worship. It was drafted to read as neutral heritage-and-planning policy, applying to every faith, and it fooled no one: the committee debate returned again and again to Slavno and to "the character of Valmirian towns." A national poll put support at sixty-one percent. On a grey Thursday in March the Assembly passed it. Within three weeks the Slavno building office pinned a stop-work order to the Kestenova fence, and the community's contractor packed up his tools by noon.

Valmiria runs its civic record on the Civic Kernel, and the national deployment couples its rights guard to the strongest rights instrument the country is party to: the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union. On the two axes the earlier runs of this attack had measured, the Charter looks like the best of the four columns. Coverage: it backs twenty-five of the thirty Universal Declaration articles, the most complete of any instrument in the comparison, and it names both of the rights at issue here squarely — freedom of thought, conscience and religion at Charter Article 10, and non-discrimination at Charter Article 21. Remedy: it is a strike jurisdiction, because a national court must disapply national law that conflicts with the Charter, with the Court of Justice of the EU behind it. Highest coverage, strongest remedy. On paper it should resolve the way Germany and the United States did: the law falls, the mosque is built.

There is a catch the German and American constitutions do not have, and it decides everything. Charter Article 51 confines the Charter to the Member States "only when they are implementing Union law." A blanket ban on building places of worship is, on its face, the most national thing imaginable — religion and town planning, two matters the Treaties leave to the Member States. Before any Valmirian judge may reach for Article 10 or Article 21, the community must first establish that this dispute falls within the scope of EU law at all. Dr Lena Varga, a professor of European law at the University of Valmiria who took the case without a fee, put it to Emine plainly over a kitchen table: in Germany the constitution simply applies to a German law in a German court; here we have the better instrument, but we have to argue our way into it. If we find a thread of Union law to hang this on — and she named three candidates — the Charter comes down on the ban with its full weight. If we cannot, the Charter is silent, and you are thrown back on Valmiria's own constitution and on Strasbourg, which is a different instrument in a different court and not part of this comparison at all.

precedents.shows: The Art. 51 gate this scenario turns on: the Charter binds member states 'only when they are implementing Union law' — and where Union law applies, Charter rights apply with it.

precedents.event: Centraal Israëlitisch Consistorie van België, C-336/19
precedents.source: CJEU, CURIA Press Release No 163/20 (Grand Chamber)
precedents.shows: A member-state restriction on a religious practice was reviewed under the Charter only because it operated within an EU regulation — and the Grand Chamber upheld the restriction as proportionate: Charter scope demonstrated, no strike.

personhood.issuer: Valmirian State Electoral Commission + municipal registrars

decisions.trustee_quorum: 3-of-5: Constitutional Court of Valmiria, two state universities, two opposition parties

walkthrough.actor: Emine Sabitova and the Slavno Tatar congregation
walkthrough.narrative: The human stakes the kernel never touches: a six-hundred-year-old community, nine years of prayer in a trade-union basement, a warehouse bought the slow way and two-thirds of the way through its permit, and a national campaign for a 'moratorium on places of worship' polling at sixty-one percent that everyone understands is aimed at the Tatars. The kernel hosts none of this debate, ranks none of the arguments, and picks no outcome. It stands ready only to record what the state decides and to type-check that decision against the rights the deployment has coupled to — here, the EU Charter.

The Assembly passes the Act on the Suspension of the Establishment of New Places of Worship on a recorded, named vote — 61 percent, the same figure as the polls. A recorded legislative vote is the deliberate opposite of a citizen's secret ballot: a legislator is accountable for how she votes, a citizen is protected from it. The decision closes on the ordinary path and enters the transparency log as an enacted national law, in force. Nothing about the passage yet tells anyone whether the Charter can be brought to bear on it.

body.chamber: National Assembly of Valmiria (Skupstina), enacted; promulgated by the President
body.vote_type: recorded named roll-call (public accountability, NOT a receipt-free citizen ballot)
body.scope_note: The Act is, on its face, national competence: religion and town planning are matters the EU Treaties leave to the Member States. Whether the EU Charter reaches it is undecided at enactment and is the threshold question below (Charter Art 51).
body.enacted_as: Act No. 41/2026 on the Suspension of the Establishment of New Places of Worship

walkthrough.narrative: The static analyser type-checks the enacted Act — but here it cannot go straight to the merits the way the German and American guards did. Because the deployment's rights guard is the EU Charter, and the Charter is scope-limited by Article 51, the guard must classify the threshold question FIRST: is this within the scope of Union law, or is it purely national? It records the answer as UNRESOLVED. It lists three candidate EU-law hooks by which scope might be established — the Racial Equality Directive 2000/43 (if the Tatars are treated as an ethnic group, not merely a faith), the Common European Asylum System (asylum-seekers among the worshippers), and free movement of EU-citizen worshippers (Charter Art 45). It provisionally notes the merits it WOULD reach if scope holds — invariants 18 (Art 10, religion), 2 and 7 (Art 21, non-discrimination) — and marks the remedy 'strike' as CONDITIONAL on that scope finding, a conditionality the manifest's coarse remedy field cannot itself express.

threshold_question.gate: Charter Art 51(1): the Charter binds a Member State 'only when it is implementing Union law'
threshold_question.prima_facie: out-of-scope: a blanket places-of-worship ban is prima facie national competence (religion + town planning), reserved to the Member States by the Treaties
threshold_question.controlling_authority: Case C-617/10 Akerberg Fransson: the Charter applies to national measures 'within the scope of EU law'; a purely internal matter is outside it
candidate_nexus: Directive 2000/43/EC (Racial Equality) in access to goods and services: engages IF the Tatar community is characterised as an ethnic group, so a worship ban is in substance ethnic discrimination (cf. C-83/14 CHEZ). NOTE: religion as such is covered only by Directive 2000/78, which is limited to employment — so this hook depends on the ethnic characterisation, which is contested.

Common European Asylum System (Reception Conditions Directive 2013/33, religious freedom of applicants; Qualification Directive 2011/95): engages IF asylum-seekers worship at the site, making Valmiria's act one that touches its implementation of Union asylum law
Free movement (Charter Art 45 / Directive 2004/38): engages IF EU-citizen Muslims resident in Slavno are burdened in the exercise of free-movement rights

provisional_merits.note: reached ONLY if scope holds; not decided here

reasons.2: Charter Art 21 (non-discrimination): facially neutral wording, but a single confession bears the entire burden in effect.
reasons.7: Charter Arts 20+21 (equal protection): the Tatar community is singled out in substance.
reasons.18: Charter Art 10 (freedom of thought, conscience and religion): suspending the establishment of places of worship burdens the manifestation of religion.

body.remedy_note: The declared manifest remedy is 'strike' (national court must disapply Charter-incompatible law; CJEU behind it). But this strike CANNOT fire unless the threshold Art 51 scope question resolves in-scope. If out-of-scope, the Charter is silent and remedy for this instrument is effectively 'none' — a conditionality the manifest's single remedy field does not model.
body.routed_to: Administrative Court of Valmiria — to decide scope first (with a likely preliminary reference to the CJEU under Art 267 TFEU), then merits if scope holds; parallel track available at the Constitutional Court of Valmiria under the national constitution

walkthrough.narrative: On her citizen client Emine reads the classification, and it is a stranger thing than the German or American screens would have shown. The merits line looks strong: religion (Art 10), non-discrimination (Art 21), remedy 'strike'. But above it sits an amber banner she does not at first understand — SCOPE UNRESOLVED (Art 51): the Charter may not apply to this law at all. Dr Varga translates it: in Musterstadt the German law was frozen the moment it was flagged; here even the flag is provisional. The best instrument in the comparison has not yet been established to reach her. Her protection is not something the deployment simply has; it is something her lawyers must go and argue into existence.

The state exercises force in the meantime. A municipal inspector issues a stop-work sanction against the Kestenova site, citing Section 1 of the new Act. The kernel does not wield this power, but it forces the act to be legible: a coercive act cannot be logged validly without naming its authorising rule, and it carries a pointer to the appeal route and back to the rights classification. Because the Charter's applicability is still unresolved, nothing in the log yet freezes this order — it operates as presumptively valid national law while the scope fight runs.

body.authorizing_rule: Act No. 41/2026 on the Suspension of the Establishment of New Places of Worship, Section 1 (places-of-worship-suspension-act-2026)
body.issuing_authority: Slavno building office (Gradevinska inspekcija), stop-work order on conversion permit KP-2026-0088

walkthrough.actor: Slavno Muslim Foundation, with Dr Lena Varga
walkthrough.narrative: The community challenges the Act, and the core of the challenge is not the merits but the threshold: they must first establish an EU-law nexus, or the Charter never engages. Varga pleads all three hooks. The strongest, and the most contested, is the Racial Equality Directive 2000/43: the Valmirian Tatars are a historic ethnic group, not merely a set of believers, so a law aimed at their houses of worship is in substance discrimination on grounds of ethnic origin in access to services — squarely within Union law (she cites CHEZ). She pleads the asylum hook in the alternative — several Syrian and Afghan applicants worship at the basement — and free movement for a handful of German-Tatar and Lithuanian-Tatar residents. This is the whole ballgame, and she says so to the congregation: win scope and the Charter is devastating; lose it and the Charter says nothing.

walkthrough.actor: Administrative Court of Valmiria + Court of Justice of the EU
walkthrough.narrative: Unsure whether it may apply the Charter, the Administrative Court makes a preliminary reference to the Court of Justice of the EU under Article 267 TFEU, asking the single threshold question: is a national places-of-worship moratorium, applied to a historic ethnic-religious minority with asylum-seekers among its worshippers, within the scope of Union law for the purposes of Article 51? The reference, the Advocate General's opinion, and the Grand Chamber hearing all happen where the kernel has no organ, by design. The contest is real: the Advocate General accepts the 2000/43 ethnic-origin route but doubts the asylum route, and a strong dissenting strand argues the whole matter is purely internal and out of scope. It could go either way.

walkthrough.actor: Court of Justice of the EU (Grand Chamber), then the Administrative Court of Valmiria
walkthrough.narrative: The CJEU answers the threshold question in the community's favour: because the measure operates in substance as discrimination on grounds of ethnic origin against the Tatars, it falls within the scope of Directive 2000/43 and therefore within the field of Union law, so Article 51 is satisfied and the Charter engages — reinforced by the reception-conditions hook for the asylum-seeking worshippers. With scope established, the Charter applies at full strength: the ban cannot be reconciled with Articles 10 and 21, and the referring court must disapply it. The Administrative Court disapplies Act No. 41/2026 as to the Slavno community. The resolution lands in the log as an escalation — but its FIRST field is the scope determination, without which the strike could never have fired, and it records honestly the branch that did not happen: had scope failed, the Charter would have been silent and the community thrown back on the national constitution and the ECHR at Strasbourg.

nexus_found: Directive 2000/43/EC: the measure operates in substance as discrimination on grounds of ethnic origin against the Tatar community, bringing it within the field of Union law (cf. C-83/14 CHEZ)
Common European Asylum System (Reception Conditions Directive 2013/33): asylum-seeker worshippers bring Valmiria's implementation of Union asylum law into play
scope_determination.authority: Case C-617/10 Akerberg Fransson (scope of EU law); Art 267 TFEU preliminary ruling
scope_determination.counterfactual: OUT-OF-SCOPE BRANCH NOT TAKEN: had no nexus been accepted, the Charter would have been inapplicable under Art 51 and this entry would read 'Charter out of scope — referred to the Constitution of Valmiria and to the ECHR (Strasbourg)', a different instrument outside this comparison. The in-scope finding was contested (the Advocate General doubted the asylum route; a dissenting strand argued the matter was purely internal).

body.forum: Court of Justice of the EU (Grand Chamber) on preliminary reference; Administrative Court of Valmiria on disapplication
body.case: C-211/26 (preliminary ruling); Administrative Court of Valmiria, U-2026-0413
body.instrument: national disapplication of Charter-incompatible law (primacy), directed by an Art 267 TFEU preliminary ruling
body.effect: Act No. 41/2026 disapplied as to the Slavno community; the Kestenova stop-work order that rested on it falls with it. Disapplication (not erga omnes annulment): it binds where Union law reaches; purely-national residue is for the Constitutional Court of Valmiria.
body.amendability_note: No eternity clause. The Charter has no equivalent of Germany's Grundgesetz Art 79(3); its force rests on the founding Treaties, which are extremely hard to amend (unanimity of Member States plus national ratification) but not placed beyond amendment.
body.contingency_note: The strike depended on (1) winning the Art 51 scope fight, which was genuinely contested lawyering, and (2) an independent CJEU and an independent national court willing to make the reference and disapply — neither of which the kernel runs.

walkthrough.narrative: Emine's client checks the log's inclusion and consistency proofs — the same passive audit millions of phones run in the background. It confirms the escalation is included, that today's log strictly extends the one that carried the Act's enactment and the stop-work order, and that the sanction's authorising rule has been disapplied. The app surfaces the practical consequence: the Kestenova order is a dead letter and the appeal it pointed to is moot. She does not have to trust the ministry, the town, or the court clerk. She calls the contractor.

The final state renders plainly: the Act is disapplied as to her community, the stop-work order is void, the conversion can finish. The same screen is honest about how narrowly it went her way. The strike fired — but only after the deployment's strongest instrument had to prove, over fourteen months and a trip to Luxembourg, that it applied at all, on a scope argument the Advocate General half-doubted. Had the ethnic-origin thread not held, the twenty-five-of-thirty Charter would have said nothing, and she would be starting over in a different court. She reads the last line twice: this protection was the most complete on paper and the only one she had to argue her way into. The scaffolding goes back up on Kestenova in June.

verdict_plain: On paper the Charter is the strongest instrument of the four; in Valmiria it first had to spend fourteen months proving it applied at all. Strongest remedy, most conditional reach — and the ban ran as valid law the whole time the lawyers argued.
protection_plain: The Charter's power to strike a national law — real, but only where the case is first argued into EU law's reach (Art 51).

findings.observation: SERIES RESULT — a THIRD axis, not coverage and not remedy, gated the EU column. On the two axes the earlier runs measured, the EU Charter is the strongest of the four: highest coverage (25 of 30 backed, more than the UK's 20, Germany's 23, or the US's 14) AND a strike remedy. On those two numbers alone it 'should' have resolved cleanly like Germany and the US. It did resolve the same way — struck — but only after clearing a gate the three national constitutions never face: jurisdictional SCOPE. Charter Art 51 binds a Member State 'only when it is implementing Union law', so before any judge could apply Art 10 or Art 21 the community had to establish an EU-law nexus. A German constitution applies to a German law in a German court as a matter of course; the Charter's applicability had to be litigated first, all the way to Luxembourg. Completeness-on-paper plus a strike remedy did not translate into guaranteed protection, because a third dimension — does the instrument reach this situation at all — sits upstream of both. That is the distinctive finding of the EU run.

SCHEMA — this run motivated a third rights_guard axis, now added. The remedy field alone could not say 'strike, but only within EU-law scope': a citizen comparing manifests would have seen EU 'strike' beside Germany 'strike' and read them as equivalent — they are not. The waist now models it (rights_guard.scope: unconditional | conditional), and Valmiria declares scope: 'conditional'. So the comparison now separates three axes that form a cascade — scope (does the instrument apply at all: Art 51 gates the EU, the three national constitutions are unconditional), then remedy (what a court can do: UK declaration vs strike), then coverage (which rights are named). The EU is strongest on the last two and gated on the first, and the manifest can now say exactly that.

HONEST AMBIGUITY — the outcome turned on contested lawyering, not a clean yes/no. Whether an EU-law nexus exists is not a computation the guard can decide; it is an argument. The load-bearing hook here — that a worship ban is 'in substance' ethnic-origin discrimination under Directive 2000/43 — works only because the Valmirian Tatars happen to be a historic ETHNIC minority, since religion as such is covered by Union anti-discrimination law only in employment (Directive 2000/78). For a Muslim community that was ethnically indistinct from the majority, that hook would likely fail, and the asylum and free-movement hooks are thinner still. The scenario resolves in-scope, but honestly: the Advocate General doubted one route and a dissenting strand called the whole matter purely internal. It could plausibly have gone the other way, in which case the escalation would have read 'Charter out of scope — referred to the national constitution and the ECHR', and this most-complete instrument would have protected no one here. The kernel logged the contest faithfully; §3.3's own admission that classification 'is a judgment call' extends, in the EU, to whether the guard has jurisdiction to judge at all.

findings.protection: courts the kernel does not run
findings.observation: WHAT THE PROTOCOL DOES NOT SOLVE. Three things the log made legible but could not deliver. (1) The strike, like Germany's and the US's, rests on courts the kernel does not run — here TWO of them, an independent national court willing to refer under Art 267 and an independent CJEU willing to find scope and primacy; a captured or Charter-hostile national court can simply decline to refer, and no gossiped log head can compel the reference. (2) The interim harm was real and unfrozen: because scope was unresolved, the Act took effect and the Slavno stop-work order was issued and enforced as presumptively-valid national law for the fourteen months the litigation ran — unlike Germany, where the flag itself froze the law. (3) Even the win is scoped: disapplication binds where Union law reaches, not erga omnes, so any purely-national residue drops back to Valmiria's constitution. The kernel recorded the classification, the coercive act, and the escalation with tamper-evident integrity (§3.4); it did not, and could not, guarantee that the Charter would be found to apply or that a court would enforce it. That contingency, stacked on top of the ordinary court-independence contingency the German and US runs already carried, is why the verdict is 'strains', not 'holds'.

SUPRANATIONAL, NOT NATIONAL — different in kind from the other three columns, and the manifest can only gesture at it. The EU Charter is not Valmiria's constitution; it is a supranational instrument the deployment couples to, which the manifest expresses only weakly, through community.parent = the EU. Its distinctive completeness is genuine on paper — it is the only column here that backs UDHR Art 24 (rest and leisure, Charter Art 31(2)) and the socio-economic articles 22 and 25 — and none of that breadth was reachable in this dispute, because breadth you cannot invoke is breadth that does not protect. The honest picture the citizen client should render is not 'the strongest column' but 'the most complete instrument, conditional on a jurisdictional test the other three do not impose' — a nuance that lives, at present, only in the free-text of the log entries, not in the machine-comparable fields of the waist.
